metadata.summary: Self-reinforcing cycles where user actions create shareable artifacts. K = i * c (invites/user times conversion). Seven types: inherent, WOM, incentivized, social, collaborative, content, embedded.

content.title: Viral Loops and Growth Mechanics
content.summary: Viral loops are self-reinforcing growth cycles where a user action creates an artifact that is shared or exposed, motivating new users to join and repeat the action. The K-factor (K = i * c, invites-per-user times conversion rate) quantifies loop strength. Seven loop types exist: inherent, word-of-mouth, incentivized, social, collaborative, content, and embedded. Select one primary loop type matched to your product before engineering, then instrument it and iterate via viral-optimization.

why.p: Linear growth (spend money → get users, stop → growth stops) caps the business at acquisition budget. A viral loop with K = 0.3 reduces effective CAC by 30% permanently and compounds over time. Even K well below 1 is valuable; K = 1+ is rare and unstable. Cycle time is as important as K: K = 0.4 with a 3-day cycle outcompounds K = 0.7 with a 60-day cycle over a quarter.

list.item: Designing a growth loop for a new product or vertical (strategic design layer).
Choosing among loop types: which fits product DNA, ICP, and current PMF stage?
Planning measurement infrastructure (events, K-funnel, cycle-time tracker) before engineering.
Companion to viral-optimization (the iterating layer) and ops-churn-prevention (retention side).
Designing or auditing a growth loop where user output (content, invites, embedded artifacts) creates distribution that brings new users.
Strategic-level work: matching loop to product type, sizing the realistic K for the category, planning measurement infrastructure.
Pre-PMF: weak product value means weak inviter motivation and the loop fizzles regardless of mechanics. Fix value, then design loops.
Pure sales-led businesses where unit economics already work — adding viral complexity gives marginal lift.
Regulated categories (finance, health, gambling) where unsolicited referrals violate FTC/MiFID II/HIPAA or CASL/GDPR compliance rules.
Products where sharing imposes social cost on the inviter (sensitive niches) — virality is anti-loyalty.

section.name: seven-loop-types
section.p: Seven viral loop types, their mechanics, canonical examples, and a product-type match guide for selecting the right primary loop.

list.item: Inherent: product requires or fails to deliver core value without inviting others (Slack, Zoom). Test: does the product fail to work without the invitee?
Word of mouth: users talk about the product unprompted (Apple, Notion). Requires a strong product story; cannot be engineered directly.
Incentivized: explicit rewards for referrals — cash, credits, features (Dropbox +500MB, Uber credits). Measurable, tunable, fraud-prone.
Social: user output is visible to non-users, creating FOMO (Spotify Wrapped, Wordle). Requires shareable artifacts.
Collaborative: product improves or requires collaboration, pulling in teammates (Figma, Notion docs). Natural for B2B / team tools.
Content: users create content that carries branding and CTAs (Canva designs, TikTok). Requires user-generated artifacts with embedded attribution.
Embedded: product name appears in output ("Made with X", "Sent via X"). Low friction, low K, good for awareness at scale.

section.name: product-type-match
section.p: Matching loop types to product categories.

list.item: B2C social / media: Social + Content loops
B2B team tools: Collaborative + Inherent loops
Productivity SaaS: Embedded + Word of mouth loops
Marketplace: Inherent + Incentivized loops
Consumer subscription (low viral): Incentivized loop as primary

rule.statement: Pick ONE primary loop type and instrument it completely before designing a second; multi-loop products without a baseline K cannot diagnose regressions.
rule.rationale: Adding a second loop before the first is measured creates attribution confusion — you cannot tell which loop is driving new users or why K changed.

section.name: loop-anatomy
section.p: Every loop has five structural elements. Define all five before engineering:

list.item: Action: what the user does that triggers the loop (completes a task, creates a document, achieves a milestone)
Artifact: what is created or shared as output (badge, report, invite link, branded content)
Distribution: how the artifact reaches potential new users (social share, email, in-product, search-indexable)
Motivation: why a new user would want to join after seeing the artifact (FOMO, utility, social connection)
Friction: steps to join from artifact exposure (target: one-click signup from invite link)

section.name: k-factor-expectations
section.p: K-factor category benchmarks to anchor expectations before designing.

rule.statement: Anchor K-factor expectations to category benchmarks before designing — consumer apps: K=0.1–0.3; B2B SaaS: K=0.05–0.2. Designing for K > 0.5 leads to dark-pattern mechanics.
rule.rationale: K > 1 is rare and unstable; most successful products operate at K = 0.1–0.4 and use the viral lift to reduce CAC rather than achieve exponential growth.

section.name: cycle-time-lever
section.p: Cycle time is a primary lever equal in importance to invites-per-user (i) and conversion rate (c).

rule.statement: Measure cycle time (p50 days from share to invitee activation) and treat it as a lever equal in importance to i and c — halving cycle time roughly squares compounding over a quarter.
rule.rationale: K = 0.4 with 3-day cycle time produces more users in 90 days than K = 0.7 with 60-day cycle time.

list.item: Reduce cycle time: one-click signup for invitees, social login, pre-filled email from invite token
Monitor cycle_time_p50 weekly; sudden increase signals friction introduced by a signup or onboarding change

section.name: invitee-landing-page-priority
section.p: The invitee landing page is the highest-priority optimization surface — most loops fail at conversion, not at sharing.

rule.statement: Treat the invitee landing page as the highest-priority optimization surface — most loops fail at conversion, not at sharing.
rule.rationale: Improving the landing page conversion rate multiplies every future loop turn; share-side improvements only increase volume into the current conversion rate.

list.item: Show inviter name and avatar prominently
Display value preview before requiring signup
One-click signup (Google/Apple SSO or email magic link)
Pre-fill email address from invite token
Mobile-optimize: target <= 3 taps from invite link to activated account

section.name: friction-reduction
section.p: Minimize friction on the invitee side to maximize conversion rate (c in K = i * c).

rule.statement: Never require signup to view shared content; a preview wall converts better than a hard gate.
rule.rationale: Hard gates drop invitee conversion by 40–60%; previewing the value before requiring signup is the correct pattern for most content and social loops.

section.name: embedded-branding
section.p: Rules for embedding branding in user-generated output and shared artifacts.

rule.statement: Embed branding in user output only where it adds value to the recipient (source credit, attribution) — not as a tax that degrades the artifact.
rule.rationale: Embedded branding used as a watermark without value to the recipient is perceived as spam; watermarks with source credit (Canva designs, Notion exports) are accepted because they provide context.

rule.statement: Plan to redesign embedded-branding loops every 12–18 months; users learn the pattern and conversion decays as familiarity removes novelty.
rule.rationale: Loops are not permanent — the pattern becomes ambient within 12–18 months and the invitee motivation ("what is this?") disappears.

section.name: case-studies
section.p: Three canonical viral loop case studies analyzed by loop anatomy and key success factors.

example.title: Dropbox: Incentivized Loop
example.note: Loop anatomy: user needs more storage → refers a friend → friend signs up → both get 500MB → friend needs more storage → loop repeats. Key factors: clear two-sided value exchange, obvious placement, instant reward delivery, shareable link. Result: 60% of signups from referrals, 35% permanent increase in signups. Note: contact-import element of this model is blocked by iOS/Android post-2021.

example.title: Spotify Wrapped: Social Loop
example.note: Loop anatomy: user listens to music all year → Spotify creates personalized annual summary → user shares on social → friends see it and want their own → non-users download Spotify. Key factors: zero effort to create the artifact, designed for social sharing, FOMO for non-users, annual ritual creates predictable spike. Result: millions of social shares, significant December download spike.

example.title: Notion Templates: Content Loop
example.note: Loop anatomy: power user creates a template → shares publicly in Notion gallery or community → others find template via search → need Notion to use it → some become template creators. Key factors: user-generated content, SEO-friendly (indexed by Google), real standalone utility, clear attribution. Loop is SEO-compounding: templates accumulate in search over time.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use subagents at design time, not at runtime. The flow: agent reads product brief → maps to loop-type taxonomy → drafts loop anatomy (action / artifact / distribution / motivation / friction) → estimates plausible K range from category benchmarks → proposes a measurement plan (events + funnel + cycle-time tracker). A human PM and growth lead approve before engineering. Iteration happens under viral-optimization.

Recommended subagents: growth-marketer (opus for strategic design, sonnet for drafts) for loop selection and design; product-strategist (opus) to match loop to product DNA and PMF stage; data-analyst (sonnet) to define K-funnel events and cycle-time measurement; legal-reviewer (opus) to flag CASL / GDPR / FTC issues in incentivized or contact-import loops.
section.prompt: Input: product brief (what it does, who pays, ICP, current GTM) Task: 1) recommend ONE primary loop type with a one-paragraph fit argument 2) draft loop anatomy: action -> artifact -> distribution -> motivation -> friction 3) estimate realistic K range citing 2 comparable products 4) list 5 events to instrument before launch Output: markdown using the README "Loop: <Name>" template Forbidden: claiming K > 0.5 without a comparable consumer-social precedent

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: CLI tools and services for viral loop instrumentation and management.

list.item: branch.io CLI — Deferred-deeplink attribution for cross-platform invites (https://help.branch.io)
posthog CLI — Funnel + cohort instrumentation for loop measurement (npm i -g posthog-node)
growthbook CLI — A/B test loop variants once shipped (npm i -g @growthbook/cli)
viral-loops API — Templated referral loop scaffolding (https://docs.viral-loops.com)
dbt-core — Materialize K-factor models in warehouse (pip install dbt-core)

section.name: services
section.p: Agent-friendly SaaS and OSS services for viral loop management and attribution.

list.item: Viral Loops (SaaS, Yes REST) — Templated campaigns: waitlist, referral, milestone
GrowSurf (SaaS, Yes REST) — Lightweight referral / waitlist
Friendbuy (SaaS, Yes REST) — Two-sided referral, fraud controls
ReferralCandy (SaaS, Yes REST) — Ecommerce referral, Shopify-native
Branch (SaaS, Yes REST + SDKs) — Invite link attribution across web + mobile
AppsFlyer / Adjust (SaaS, Yes REST) — Mobile attribution incl. SKAdNetwork
Loops.so / Customer.io (SaaS, Yes REST) — Trigger lifecycle messages from loop events
PostHog (OSS + SaaS, Yes REST + HogQL) — Funnel + retention for loop measurement

section.name: loop-projection-script
section.p: Back-of-envelope loop projection (compounding model) to show whether assumed K + cycle yields self-sustaining growth.

list.item: Pick one primary loop and instrument it deeply before adding a second; multi-loop products without baseline K cannot debug regressions.
Cycle time is a primary lever — halving cycle time roughly squares the compounded effect over a quarter.
Embed branding in user output where it adds value to the recipient (link preview, watermark with credit) rather than as a tax.
Tie inviter rewards to invitee value-realization, not invitee signup, to push fraud below noise.
Treat the invitee landing page as a top-priority surface — most loops fail at conversion, not at sharing.
Re-design loops every 12–18 months; users learn the pattern and conversion rate (c) decays.

section.name: limitations
section.p: Known failure modes and measurement limitations for viral loops.

list.item: K > 1 is rare and unstable; designing for it leads to dark-pattern shares that backfire.
Loop math assumes invitees behave like inviters — they often retain worse, especially in incentivized loops.
"Embedded branding" loops (Made-with-X) decay as the brand becomes ambient; require continuous design refresh.
Cross-platform attribution (web → mobile, email → app) is broken by ITP, mail-open prefetch, and iOS Mail Privacy Protection — your K may be under-counted by 30–60%.
Forced sharing (gated content) drives short-term K but kills long-term retention and brand.
LLMs over-cite Dropbox / Spotify Wrapped / Notion as if any product can clone them. Force comparable-precedent matching by stage and category.

"Inherent" virality is rare — agents will label collaborative-tool features as inherent when they are merely incentivized. Demand the inherent-loop test: "does the product fail to deliver core value without inviting someone?"
Agents propose mechanics that violate platform TOS (auto-DM friends, contact upload without consent). Filter at template level.
K-projection math depends on invitee retention; agent models that assume invitee behaves like inviter overstate compounding by 2–5x.
Generated copy invents social proof ("join 100,000 people") that is factually wrong; numeric claims must be passed in, not produced.
Cross-platform attribution gaps invalidate K reported by single-tool dashboards — instruct the agent to specify which tool measures which leg.

antipattern.description: Agent recommends auto-DM friends, contact upload without consent, or "share with all contacts" mechanics.
antipattern.reason: These violate platform TOS (LinkedIn, Twitter), CASL, GDPR, and CCPA. Filter at template level — do not let the agent generate these mechanics.

antipattern.description: Agent labels a collaborative-tool invite flow as "inherent virality".
antipattern.reason: Inherent virality has a strict test: does the product fail to deliver core value without the invitee joining? Collaboration invites are typically incentivized or convenience-driven, not inherent.

antipattern.description: Agent invents social proof claims ("join 100,000 people") without input data.
antipattern.reason: LLMs produce plausible-sounding numbers; all numeric claims in invite copy must be passed in as inputs, not generated.

template.purpose: Loop design template: name, type, action/artifact/distribution/motivation/friction, K-factor targets.
Referral program design: value proposition, mechanics, sharing options, tracking, fraud rules.
Naive compounding model: project user count from K + cycle time over N days.
